data.title: CEF Weekly Review: Amundi Shareholders Are Refusing To Say Yes
data.description:
Welcome to another installment of our CEF Market Weekly Review, where we discuss closed-end fund [CEF] market activity from both the bottom-up, highlighting individual fund news and events as well as the top-down, providing an overview of the broader market. We also try to provide some historical context as well as the relevant themes that look to be driving markets or that investors ought to be mindful of.

This update covers the period through the third week of August. Be sure to check out our other weekly updates covering the business development company [BDC] as well as the preferreds/baby bond markets for perspectives across the broader income space.

Market Action

This past week's CEF price action was mixed. Equity-linked sectors rallied while a number of fixed-income sectors fell as longer-term Treasury yields rose. Month-to-date, CEFs are holding up well overall.

CEFs discounts remain toppy and only below levels reached in the boom time of 2021 in the past decade.

Market Themes

A pair of Pioneer/Amundi CEFs PHD and PHT have adjourned their special stockholder meetings to 20-Aug. The two funds have asked stockholders to approve the liquidation of the funds. While shareholders did approve the same plan for HNW, not enough PHD and PHT stockholders have voted, so the funds have allowed more time for more holders to vote.

This is the second time the funds have postponed the vote. The irony here is that the reason the funds are looking to liquidate is because they did not obtain a sufficient number of votes to approve a new advisory agreement when Amundi, the funds' former investment adviser, was merged with Victory Capital. The stockholders were asked to approve a new advisory agreement with Victory Capital; however, the sufficient number of votes did not materialize.

Naturally, the sensible question is, if stockholders didn’t pay enough attention to vote on the new advisory agreement, why would they pay attention to vote on a liquidation? Arguably, a liquidation does sound like a bigger deal than a new advisory agreement, so that might make more people pay attention, but that doesn’t seem to be the case.

Perhaps the funds should threaten to move into cash and delist - that might get people’s attention. For now, the discounts are around 2%. The most likely outcome is that the funds will indeed liquidate, delivering this discount amortization. However, it would be ironic if Amundi were forced to keep running the fund because investors don’t care enough to pay attention. This dynamic where shareholders can't be bothered to vote for sensible things is likely why managers are not starting new CEFs.

Market Commentary

CLO Equity CEF OXLC provided an update on its share repurchase program. The fund bought back around $17.4m worth of shares through 13-Aug. The weighted-average price of shares was $3.53 (it closed at $3.47 yesterday). The capacity of the program is $150m. The discount of the fund remains historically wide at around 18%, which suggests that it will continue to buy back shares, generating significant NAV accretion and supporting the share price. Tactically, the stock looks compelling.

The Eagle Income CLO CEFs had their earnings calls. EIC bought back $6.5m of common shares at a weighted-average discount of 6.4%, generating two cents of NAV accretion. Given the discount is now around 10%, buybacks should continue, supporting the price and driving further NAV accretion. Management said that the CLO market has been lagging the broader credit market, which is apparent in NAV performance. This does hold out some hope that the NAV declines this year do not reflect locked-in economic losses.

One aspect where ECC was different from EIC is in realized losses. ECC had realized losses that were about a third of its NII during the quarter, while EIC had a small net realized gain. The realized losses in ECC were previously classified as unrealized losses, so this may indicate a degree of lock-in for the NAV declines in CLO Equity funds, at least for ECC.

One element that has been hurting CLO Equity funds is that CLO Debt spreads have not tightened in as much as loan spreads. This means that Equity sees lower income on the asset side (i.e., the loan portfolio) of its balance sheet without much offset on the liability side (i.e., the CLO Debt).

Elsewhere, short-duration high-yield CEF BGH declared a special distribution of $0.241, or about 2 base distributions. We don’t see these very often from credit CEFs, as these tend to overdistribute rather than underdistribute. The last annual shareholder report had NII / month of $0.1325 vs. distribution of $0.1223. The retained income works out to $0.12, and much of the rest is due to the fact that lower leverage costs are not in the NII figures given the report is for 2024. As discussed earlier, limited-duration CEFs have been decent alternatives to loan CEFs as their duration is low, but they don’t take an immediate hit to income on Fed rate cuts.

data.title: ChainLink USD: Key Recent Developments Demonstrating Growth
data.description:
I'm providing an update on the cryptocurrency ChainLink USD (LINK-USD), which I wrote about back in December 2024. ChainLink increased about 17% since my article was published, significantly outperforming the S&P 500's (SPY) 9% gain. My theory back in December was that ChainLink was likely to perform well along with Bitcoin (BTC-USD) and the other main cryptocurrencies as part of this BTC halving cycle. I also pointed out ChainLink's real-life use cases as long-term growth drivers for its underlying price.

New Developments for ChainLink

ChainLink, as the standard for onchain finance, had multiple developments in 2025 that demonstrate its real-life use cases. These use cases can help drive the price of the underlying ChainLink coin higher as more investors become aware of LINK as an investment and bid up the price.

On August 11, 2025, ChainLink and Intercontinental Exchange (ICE) announced that high-quality FX and precious metals rates from ICE Consolidate Feed will serve as a contributor to the derived data set that Chainlink Data Streams provides to over 2,000 applications asset managers, leading banks, and infrastructure in the ChainLink ecosystem. This marks a milestone for ChainLink to have a path towards the adoption of mainstream onchain finance.

ICE has a market-leading data feed for numerous precious metals and currencies, which provides a new level of reliability to onchain markets. ICE also supports new types of tokenized assets and products. ICE serves as one of multiple high-quality data providers on Chainlink Data Streams, which supplies DeFi (decentralized finance) applications with access to tamper-resistant data. ChainLink's data standard handles the rigorous requirements of traditional capital markets and unlocks a new class of institutional-grade applications with ICE's data.

The demand for tokenized real-world assets is expected to reach $30.1 trillion by 2034. This market is still in its infancy and was worth about $5 billion in early 2024. Chainlink Data Streams helps organizations tap into this large opportunity by providing on-demand access to secure and reliable high-frequency market data. This allows onchain applications to leverage ChainLink's institutional-grade infrastructure. This provides fast, user-friendly experiences.

On August 7, 2025, ChainLink announced the launch of the Chainlink Reserve, which is a new upgrade of a strategic onchain reserve of LINK tokens. This is designed to support the sustainability and long-term growth of the ChainLink Network. This is accomplished by accumulating LINK tokens using offchain revenue from large enterprises that are adopting the ChainLink standard and from onchain service usage.

ChainLink Co-Founder, Sergey Nazarov, stated: "The launch of the Chainlink Reserve marks a pivotal evolution in Chainlink, establishing a strategic LINK reserve funded using offchain revenue, as well as from onchain service usage. Demand for the Chainlink standard has already created hundreds of millions of dollars in revenue, substantially from large enterprises. The Chainlink Reserve provides a clear answer to how offchain revenue and large scale institutional adoption of the Chainlink standard will be connected back to the growth, security and sustainability for those standards."

On August 4, 2025, ChainLink announced the launch of Chainlink Data Streams for U.S. Equities and ETFs. This is real-time, high-throughput market data that is now available onchain to power the next generation of tokenized financial products. This is being adopted by DeFi protocols: GMX, Kamino, and GMX-Solana. Chainlink Data Streams enables tokenized stock trading, synthetic ETFs, and perpetual futures with institutional-grade reliability.

On June 30, 2025, ChainLink launched the automated compliance engine [ACE]. This was done in partnership with Apex Group, ERC-3643 Association, and GLEIF. ACE is a unified and modular standard to solve all onchain compliance issues and bring institutional capital onchain. This is designed to support traditional and decentralized finance. ACE enables the creation of compliance-focused digital assets/services across private and public blockchains. This sets up the potential for over $100 trillion of institutional capital to enter the onchain economy.

ChainLink partnered with Mastercard (MA) in June to enable over 300 billion cardholders to purchase crypto directly onchain. This is accomplished through a secure fiat-to-crypto conversion. This combines Mastercard's trusted global payments network with ChainLink's secure interoperability infrastructure. This development removes barriers so that mainstream users can access the onchain economy.

World Liberty Financial collaborated with ChainLink in May to accelerate the adoption of the world's fastest-growing stablecoin, USD1. This involves the integration of ChainLink's Cross Chain Interoperability Protocol [CCIP] with World Liberty Financial to expand access to the institutional-grade stablecoin by enabling secure transfers of USD1 across blockchain networks that would otherwise act as silos of value. ChainLink enables USD1 to move seamlessly between Ethereum (ETH-USD) and BNB Chain networks with a rigorous plan for additional blockchain integrations in the future.

Those were most of the recent developments. However, there were others from earlier in the year that can be reviewed from the first link 'multiple developments' in this section.

Technical Perspective

It's important to keep an eye on the technicals for LINK since we can't track earnings and valuation like a standard company. The price of ChainLink tends to be correlated with Bitcoin's halving cycle. Since there are sharp drops in each cycle, I think it is wise to lock in profits near the peak price in every cycle. Then, investors can get back in near the price bottoms. It might be difficult to time it perfectly, but having a long-term view on the monthly chart makes it feasible.

The monthly chart above shows how LINK peaked in the last cycle after the price moved into an extreme overbought condition when the RSI moved above 80. What was tricky about the last cycle was that there was another pump from July through October after sharp declines in May and June.

The RSI is currently in bullish territory as it is above the 50 level but still below the overbought area above 70.

The MACD is showing a bullish crossover as the blue line crossed above the red signal line. This is confirmed by the green histogram bar appearing after the recent red bars. This shows that a new positive trend has formed. This suggests that there could be more upside runway for LINK over the next few months.

Selling when the monthly RSI moves above 80 is probably a good time to lock in profits. Profit-taking tended to take place at this level each time that occurred. This can be cross-checked with the MACD as the histogram bars turn from dark green to light green and shorten in length. The buying point after the last cycle occurred when the RSI moved down to about the 40 level and when the MACD histogram bars turned from red to pink and shortened in length. This was when the price of LINK dropped to the single digits.

Risks for Investing in ChainLink

There is no guarantee that the past patterns for LINK will repeat like they did in the past. So, it might be wise to use the price action of Bitcoin as a guide, which tends to have more consistent patterns. My latest Bitcoin analysis can be read here.

Of course, there is no guarantee that using Bitcoin as a guide for price peaks and bottoms will repeat the patterns of past cycles. Investing is about weighing risks while attempting to maximize gains. So, sometimes the higher-risk assets yield higher total returns, but it could also lead to losses.

ChainLink could lose ground due to competition. It is possible that one or more of ChainLink's competitors offers more attractive technology that is adopted by large players. ChainLink competes with Pyth (PYTH-USD), Chronicle, Switchboard, and WINkLink (WCoin USD) (WIN-USD). I doubt that these competitors will overtake ChainLink in this space, but the possibility is there.

ChainLink's Outlook

I like the numerous real-world developments that ChainLink is involved with. ChainLink has partnerships with many large companies and organizations. This shows that LINK is a leader in the space with the potential for strong future growth.

I will watch for the RSI to peak again in the overbought area on the RSI and loss of momentum on the MACD (shortening of the green bars) to gauge a selling point for this cycle. The current price action suggests that this could be the beginning of the next leg higher in this cycle.

data.title: NANO Nuclear Energy: Concepts Of A Plan With Many Patents
data.description:
Last month, Trump issued executive orders for the Department of Energy to fast-track the development of small modular nuclear reactors. The order aims for the first reactors to begin generation within a year. In theory, these reactors are lower cost and take less time to produce, potentially overcoming the primary issues with traditional reactors (high Capex and development delays). Ten companies with eleven reactor projects have been approved in the DOE streamlined authorization.

One company not currently included in that list is NANO Nuclear Energy (NASDAQ:NNE). Its CEO, Jay Yu, reacted positively to the orders. In a related news interview, he explains that Nano is looking to create a vertically integrated reactor system with fuel enrichment, claiming his company has the only US patent to produce fuel using lasers. Nano is much earlier in the development timeline than peers like Oklo (OKLO), which I am speculatively bullish on.

Nano differs from those in the executive order as it is focusing on microreactors, not small reactors, producing less than 20 MW of power (whereas SMRs may produce up to 300 MW). Nano's reactor designs ZEUS and ODIN should produce 1-1.5MW, roughly enough power for about 800-1,000 homes or one small data center or a medium hospital. It is also looking at mining as a key market, given the remote locations of mines and high energy needs.

In theory, this reactor could be carried by a standard semi-truck, producing "constant power for at least 10 full power years." It also has a larger 10-ton LOKI designed to output up to 5 MW, still highly transportable. Finally, its largest project is the KRONOS system, which it acquired in January, aiming to create up to 45MW of power in a stationary underground site.

NANO Nuclear Energy is a pre-revenue company. It likely has around fifty employees, making it early in its development timeline. It may be leading the race in microreactors, but this is a small niche with a technology that has not yet proven commercially viable. I tend to agree with the opinion that smaller nuclear projects may be the primary means by which the US raises power generation to meet rising demand (after decades of stagnant power demand and low utility investment). That said, SMR and microreactor technologies are still unproven for commercial viability, and NANO is arguably not concentrated enough on any one product.

The Race to Criticality is Accelerating

NANO has big promises but minimal physical assets or deals that guarantee profitable success. Its valuation must not only account for the potential income it can earn but also be discounted for the risk that it fails to deliver on its lofty aims. Many of these risks relate to the development timeline and costs, and I question firms that either overpromise or have no path to profitability within 5-10 years.

NANO is currently finishing up the R&D stage. It has "six utility patent applications pending before the USPTO," and "nine patent applications related to our acquired ALIP technology for small nuclear reactor cooling." It also has licenses for fuel transportation. Its medium reactor project, LOKI, was anticipated to begin testing in 2026, but this was seemingly pushed back to 2027. This is the LOKI MMR project, which is designed for deep space missions.

NANO's primary projects are the ODIN and ZEUS microreactors. From its investor presentation, NANO markets these reactors as having better economies of scale, lower safety risk, and true modular transportability than both traditional reactors and SMRs. Most of that presentation focuses on the general advantage of nuclear over other energy sources, the steps for enriching uranium fuel, the R&D and initial development of the large KRONOS project, and, of course, bipartisan support for legislation and orders for nuclear development. All of those points are good, but we see limited information regarding the ODIN and ZEUS projects.

In its prospectus, it noted:

We estimate that our microreactor demonstration work will be conducted between 2024 and 2026, our microreactor licensing application will be processed between 2026 and 2031, and our microreactors will be launched between 2030 and 2031.

It is currently doing demonstration projects, assembling a 1:2 scale block that represents a fuel element of the ZEUS reactor core. The company is then testing the block to show its thermo-nuclear properties. That is not a nuclear test, but simply heating a scaled model to show how it distributes and handles heat.

In my view, investors must understand that NNE is years behind OKLO, which is aiming for commercial operations by 2027. By then, NNE may be working on licensing, which may process faster than expected in 2024 due to Trump's fast-tracking orders. Still, I highly doubt we'll see commercial projects before 2030. By then, its larger competitors may be encroaching on its projects, having a potential edge in physical development after their SMRs are completed. Of course, that may also make NNE an acquisition target due to its intellectual property focus.

What is NANO Nuclear Energy Worth?

When I look at Nano, I can't help but remember Nikola (OTC:NKLAQ), which, in 2020, I saw as a "publicly traded Kickstarter campaign" that basically had designs and no viable path to commercial success. It is now worth just 3 cents, having failed to meet its basic aims.

Nano is different in that it's not promising to reinvent the wheel (a basic Tesla knock-off) but create new technologies for an emerging nuclear industry that is expected to have strong demand growth in the 2030s. Still, there is no guarantee that its IP designs will pass testing. Even after that, physical development can often cost more time and money than expected. Further, even if it creates physical microreactors in the early 2030s, it is unclear if demand for such is strong, given its application is niche to more rural areas.

NNE is highly correlated with its larger peers, Oklo and NuScale (SMR). NNE's market capitalization is $1.3B, well below that of the other two but not exceptionally lower:

Ultimately, the success of these projects is determined in part by funding. If investors or the government believe in SMRs, they may fund the space enough that it will produce some commercially viable and scalable products.

Market capitalizations give us an idea of their access to capital. However, we can also compare this to the liquidity they've already received. To me, these companies' cash levels are crucial because they determine how long they can operate at a loss and provide an idea regarding investors' expectations of future potential. Oklo is leading, expected to be the closest to commercial operations, with NuScale aiming for 2030. Nano is behind, likely in the mid-to-early 2030s (after 2030-2031). The market cap-to-cash ratios of these firms reflect these timelines, with Nano having the lowest at 6.8X vs. Oklo at 15.5X:

Although there is no guarantee that NANO's projects will be commercially viable, it has $210M to find out. Its burn rate is also similar to its peers, at around 3-9% of cash used each quarter in operating losses, in the same range as Oklo and NuScale:

Combined with its market-cap-to-cash ratio, I think this demonstrates that NANO is essentially a younger version of the other two. Although it is challenging to value these firms, I believe NANO's financial position places its valuation on par with OKLO and SMR, with a discount for its youth. My speculative valuation of OKLO, based on its lower projected levelized power costs and commercial contracts, indicated it was probably undervalued and could generate "upwards of $5.6B in revenue" by the mid-2030s.

I agree with other analysts that its operational focus is too disorganized. The reason I like Oklo is that it has a clear path to profitability focused on a core SMR product that, based on its estimates, should be commercially viable. Nano has far less of this information, being earlier in its timeline, but it is still focused on too many different things. It's promising a vertically integrated fuel enrichment business, a transportation venture, consulting, medium SMRs, and small microreactors.

For me, it would be better if it could focus solely on the microreactor niche, aiming to be the first commercially viable project. Instead, it's somewhat of a "fund" or collection of intellectual property on various micro/SMR nuclear projects that may or may not be commercially viable or even technically feasible.

The Bottom Line

Nano's booming stock price has been met with increased short-interest, as have the other two. The SMR industry has very high short-interest at double-digits, with NNE's 18% between Oklo and NuScale:

Overall, this industry is extremely risky and may be overvalued after the more recent rally. For me, whenever a specific industry is receiving excess political and media attention, it may be best to avoid it due to overvaluation risk.

Detractors, such as the Environmental Working Group, which has a stated anti-nuclear agenda, see SMRs as having a natural diseconomy of scale. Basically, they state reactors produce non-linearly more power the larger they are, meaning smaller reactors will produce less power per "pound of steel." From what I understand, Oklo and Nano would disagree, given that they market themselves as having superior scale, probably due to less labor and utility maintenance needs.

EWG also argues that this technology would not be viable without tremendous supply-chain investments, which seems to be true, yet may be solved due to high investor financing interest and increased government policy support. Others also argue that SMRs are not viable due to HALEU fuel needs and limited domestic production of such.

These points are all widely debated, but it is clear that SMRs are a risky business. Until we see commercial operations, I am hesitant. Though I maintain my long-term bullish view on OKLO, my outlook on NNE is neutral. NNE seems to be valued in line with its peers, with a reasonably discount compared to its liquidity. That said, I think NNE needs to be more focused on projects that it thinks will be the most commercially viable and niche compared to its larger peers. For now, it is mostly a collection of intellectual property that may never be used or may not be viable.

That said, NNE will be worth revisiting within a year when we get more information regarding the demonstrations of its projects and potentially more support if Oklo (or others) get closer to power generation.

data.title: PotlatchDeltic: U.S. Timberland REIT Waiting For The Next Housing Upcycle
data.description:
Introduction & Financials

PotlatchDeltic Corporation (NASDAQ:PCH) is a top US timberland REIT that has been going through a lot of volatility over time as a result of it mirroring the US economy. Right now, we’re looking at some of the lowest levels over the past 5 years, and I believe this has potential to be an opportunity at the right prices, so let’s look into them.

The company owns 2.1 million acres of timberland, being a top 10 US lumber producer with 7 manufacturing facilities that make either lumber or plywood, offering significant exposure to the US economy and its housing market, both through this commodity and its own real estate development operations.

Something that sets them apart is their leverage over lumber prices. While Weyerhaeuser (WY) or Rayonier (RYN) have 29% and 0% direct leverage to lumber prices, respectively, PCH (55%) is poised to benefit even more from the long-term fundamental tailwinds of this commodity. As a result of the indexed sawlogs, their contracted prices are tied to the market price of lumber instead of a fixed cost, allowing the company to capture the full benefit of strong lumber markets but also exposing them to weaker times. As a result, they see ~$15 million in EBITDDA per $10 MBF change in lumber prices.

They also provide this graph that explains the previous nearly one decade from the perspective of lumber. Noteworthy, we’ve seen strong prices as a result of the pandemic affecting the supply chain and, to some degree, also boosting DIY demand, followed by falling housing demand as a result of higher interest rates. In response to this, we’ve seen significant curtailments that limited the supply in 2024 [see companies such as West Fraser Timber (WFG), Canfor Corporation (OTCPK:CFPZF), or Interfor Corporation (OTCPK:IFSPF)], Interfor even saying they did it to help bring their production and the needs of our customers back into balance. This is a normal aspect of the commodity cycle.

As a result of the weaker commodity prices, we can see the company’s cash flows fall from the 2020-2022 highs. They’re now expecting $60 million to $65 million in CAPEX in 2025, in line with the levels from 2019 and 2021 and 50% below the peak of $131 million in 2023 as a result of a sawmill modernization and expansion. During their latest earnings Q&A, they also stated that they expect improvements in Q3 as a result of business improvements:

We anticipate that our total adjusted EBITDDA for the third quarter will be significantly higher than our second quarter results, driven by improved performance in both Real Estate and Wood Products divisions.

Financially, based on their latest report, we can see an overall good position, with the current assets covering the current liabilities and a lot of assets that are well above their debt. We can see $1.92 billion worth of equity compared to a market cap of $3.29 billion, placing them at a PB of ~1.71, but it’s important to remember that the current value of their assets, especially given the opportunities I’ll mention below, is likely higher than when they were acquired and recorded on the balance sheet, which is an important aspect to remember.

Their debt is also well-laddered and has a 100% fixed interest rate, with no significant maturities in the near future and the potential to refinance, which should be helped by rate cuts by the time they need to do that, as I’ll mention below. As a result, they expect $35 million in interest expenses in FY 2025, which is one of their highest levels ever, being explained by their growing size combined with

Dividends & Buybacks

Regarding the dividend, we see something quite normal for a commodity company - a lot of volatility. As a note, the 2018 dividend jump is the result of their merger (Potlatch Corporation and Deltic Timber Corporation), while the jump in 2022 is the result of exceptional results thanks to the price of wood. Currently, that $1.8 per share dividend costs them a total of ~$141.1 million based on TTM data, which is significant for their cash flows in this market, although they expect improvements in the coming months.

Overall, we can see a very disciplined capital allocation strategy since 2014, with 56% of their capital going into acquisitions, 28% for dividends, 12% CAPEX, and 4% into buybacks, all while maintaining a strong balance sheet and using extra cash flow for investing, buybacks, and M&A. However, that’s not all.

Something that I have to congratulate them on is the way they do the share buybacks/issuance. As they say, this is an opportunistic process, doing buybacks during downturns (i.e., 2019-2020, 2022-2025) and issuing stocks in times when the market is exuberant, which is a very significant plus for this management team, in my opinion. Unlike other commodity companies, this is one of the very few that used the upcycle to issue stock and capitalize on the market’s hype, while the downcycles are seen as times to buy at a discount, lowering the future payable dividends (not accounting for increases), which is actually an investment, especially for a REIT, as they know they’ll have to pay those dividends.

Risks & Opportunities

On a general note, they have the same risks and opportunities I mentioned in the Rayonier stock analysis posted recently.

First of all, this biological asset comes with a very interesting perspective that’s kind of unique compared to many other commodities. Technically, their timber is inventory, but they don’t have to sell (aka cut) it if the prices aren’t attractive enough. This is similar to mining companies, for example, but by waiting, their inventory literally grows by itself, which copper or gold don’t do, as the trees also grow in size (3-5% average growth per year in general), giving PCH an optionality to delay harvest. This is basically an “on the stump” storage that makes timber more stable in price, as the supply is easily adjustable to the demand and healthy companies don’t have to rush selling at any cost.

As for the long term, as Rayonier also states, timberland companies have quite a lot of opportunities to grow their margins. First, they have the possibility of coming from carbon sequestration, credits, renewable energy (e.g., solar), etc. that can solidify very good margins on top of the current slow and steady timberlands.

Similarly, PCH also sees potential to 5-20x their timberland EBITDDA per acre from both near-term opportunities (solar, carbon credits) and also long-term ones like lithium production on their land, carbon capture and storage, and bioenergy, which could be gamechangers if they come to fruition.

Plus, as PCH also does, they can market their higher-value land to be used in real estate, together offering a whole new light to this type of business. Such properties include the Chenal Valley community in Arkansas, a large, upscale, master-planned community, as well as Red Oak Ridge, both of which reinforce the idea of monetizing the “full” value of their land, selling a portion of it for higher-value uses while maintaining their core timberland business.

As for the future of their real estate and lumber prices, there are also long-term tailwinds supporting. We’re currently looking at strong support from millennials being in the prime home buying years combined with over a decade of underbuilding and low inventory of existing homes for sale on the market.

To some degree, a counterargument to this can be the affordability of a house nowadays, especially post-pandemic, up 47.1% from 2020 to 2024, which is massive compared to the 15.87% nominal increase in median weekly earnings for full-time workers.

Still, in the near term, their number one opportunity should be rate cuts. Lower interest rates will help them directly by reducing the interest on their debt (especially variable/refinanced one in the future), but also allow them to develop in a more active environment since rate cuts will boost the economy by increasing disposable budgets and lowering the cost/burden of debt. This can be great for the real estate development side in particular, making it easier for them to develop the land, find partners, and especially clients, as lowering the interest rates also leads to lowering the cost of mortgage, making buying real estate more affordable for regular people. Right now, we’re looking at significantly higher mortgage rates from the ~4% we’ve seen a decade ago or even below 3% post-pandemic (as low as 2.65% by the start of 2021). This is completely normal, as that is one of the effects and reasons behind rate cuts, which is one of the Fed’s tools to fight inflation.

Plus, that’s also more demand for timber, so PCH would win from all sides of it. As they present, we could soon be looking at a recovery in demand, while the capacity wouldn’t really be able to keep up after the 2023 drop in demand.

Meanwhile, the price of lumber has been relatively stable since the 2021-2022 jump, which is another aspect to keep in mind about this company, as they saw exceptional cash flows during the 2021-2022 boom in prices and terrible numbers a decade ago during the downside.

And also important for the prices, one final point I made in the RYN stock analysis regarding tariffs: being a US-based producer, tariffs/trade wars/etc. - especially on Canada - could actually be great for PCH. Not only would they result in higher timber prices since Canada is a top producer and exporter, but it could also help PCH score a better premium since they’re a local producer, with the potential to see a friendlier policy towards “made in America” products. I don’t think a trade war with Canada (or anyone else, actually) is the end goal, but while they last, PCH could enjoy the environment.

Peer Comparison

As for the competition, I’ll include only Rayonier and Weyerhaeuser, as these two are the main public companies competing in their markets and with the same REIT operating model. As we can see, most valuation metrics are in line with or above the peers, so nothing that screams undervalued or overvalued, especially since we’re looking at a small set of peers due to how concentrated this market is when it comes to timberland REITs.

Valuation

Using a starting FCF of $155.6 million in 2024, I went for a 4.5% CAGR for the first 5 years to account for solid demand throughout a recovery thanks to rate cuts, a relatively modest 3.5% CAGR for the subsequent 5 years as a result of their growth potential into other industries, a 2.5% terminal growth rate, and a WACC of 6.5%, slightly below my estimated 6.92% (assuming a cost of equity of ~8% and cost of debt of ~3.5%) to account for slight rate cuts this year even though I expect more in the near future. This way, the resulting DCF valuation for PotlatchDeltic yields an enterprise value of approximately $4.70 billion. After subtracting the $939.89 million in net debt, this returns an equity value of roughly $3.76 billion. With 78.44 million shares outstanding, this gives an intrinsic value of about $47.95 per share, which is ~12.4% profit from the current level.

Conclusions

Overall, given the company’s financials and relatively high amount of debt, I rate them a Hold now, as I see no significant enough reason to go for them instead of Rayonier, which also pays a better dividend even when we exclude the special one paid recently and comes with significantly better financials. They could be a way to gain exposure to the industry, but at these prices I see better potential in other places, although it could be something to consider adding on significant dips.

data.title: D-Wave's Quantum Dream Vs. Financial Reality: Still A Sell Despite Rally
data.description:
Introduction

D-Wave Quantum’s (NYSE:QBTS) stock has rallied 130% since my last evaluation in late April. I rated the stock a Sell due to its niche opportunity in quantum annealing and a crowded and distant opportunity in gate-model quantum computing, which D-Wave recently began researching. I was also concerned with D-Wave’s financials, which feature a $40 million annual “cash burn” (cash from operations). I ended the article with some things that could change my mind on QBTS, including the development of a practical quantum advantage for annealing over traditional alternatives, sustainable revenue growth/profitability, and the successful development of a superior gate-model quantum computer.

My update below reflects a disconnect between D-Wave’s long-term technological potential and its current, early-stage financial profile, meriting continued caution.

Q2 Reality Check

Let’s start by reviewing D-Wave’s second-quarter earnings report. Revenue increased 42% YoY to $3.1 million. It’s a bit silly to read too much into the 42% YoY figure, given it is off such a tiny base. In any event, the growth is driven by D-Wave’s recent launch of the sixth-generation Advantage2 system.

The net loss in Q2 ballooned to $167.3 million, but this was driven by a $142 million non-cash charge from warrant revaluation. Excluding the one-off, D-Wave’s adjusted net loss was around $25 million (widened from $20 million a year ago).

D-Wave’s newest Advantage2 system was launched in Q2. Its 6th-generation annealing system reportedly features lower noise and a path toward 100,000 qubits. They also unveiled an open-source “Quantum AI Toolkit,” which helps developers integrate quantum methods into machine learning workflows. Unlike in Q1, D-Wave did not deliver any quantum systems, although it assembled an Advantage2 system for a U.S. defense contractor and signed an agreement for a new system in South Korea. Bear in mind that it could be a few months or so before D-Wave recognizes revenue from these two sales.

Land-and-Expand: Cloud Versus Hardware

D-Wave’s strategy is “land-and-expand.” It sells these massive machines for several million dollars and then provides professional services (e.g., its Launch program for onboarding). D-Wave is also involved in software. It provides quantum-computing-as-a-service via a cloud platform. However, this is not broken out in detail. Management does anecdotally highlight growing interest in cloud access to its annealing solvers.

Its land-and-expand strategy could work both ways. Customers interested in its quantum-computing-as-a-service could lead to larger engagements or even on-premise system purchases. However, customers would undoubtedly need very compelling reasons to spend millions on an on-premise system.

Looking ahead, D-Wave did not issue explicit quarterly or full-revenue/EPS guidance in its Q2 report, which is not uncommon for a company at this stage. It’s feasible that 2H 2025 could see another large transaction (the South Korea MOU could close as a sale). We can assume that operating expenses will remain high as D-Wave continues working on a 7th-generation annealer and a future gate-model processor.

Cash Cushion

In this light, D-Wave’s $400 million at-the-market equity offering in Q2 came at the perfect time. As of its last report, D-Wave held $819.3 million in cash, cash equivalents, and short-term investments. The company carries minimal debt. So, the ATM extends its cash runway, which is critical given high operating expenses, lumpy revenue, and management's explicit interest in M&A opportunities.

Cloud Strategy Aligns with Industry Trend

Zooming out, I don’t want to go too deep into industry trends, secular drivers, and competition, as it has been covered ad nauseam. An interesting development is the gradual adoption of quantum computing via cloud platforms (e.g., Amazon Braket, Azure Quantum), which lowers barriers to experimentation (i.e., substantial upfront costs). D-Wave’s Leap cloud service fits into this trend by providing on-demand access to its machines globally. This makes it much easier for companies like D-Wave to reach customers and generate recurrent, SaaS-like revenue. Still, the niche appeal of D-Wave’s annealing technology is just its entry into the quantum space. Management recognizes this. The real theme is the long-term rat race in quantum computing towards truly useful quantum computers.

We also have to consider D-Wave’s competitive positioning relative to its peers. D-Wave’s annealing systems, although niche, are an actual product with practical use cases. Whereas IonQ’s (IONQ) focus is on future fault-tolerant quantum computers. Larger technology companies, like IBM (IBM) and Microsoft (MSFT), are heavily invested in quantum R&D, making it difficult to imagine D-Wave making a lot of ground in gate-model quantum computers. D-Wave’s path forward, really, is maintaining a unique value proposition in practical annealing solutions and hybrid quantum-classical algorithms.

The “Dizzy” Framework

D-Wave and other quantum names are the epitome of Dizzy in the human-in-the-loop system that is the stock market. So, it’s important to establish what events should drive investment decisions. Tier-1 (Decisive) signals include earnings results and official filings, which provide critical information on growth, financial stability, and customer wins. Tier-2 (Supportive) signals include product announcements, analyst opinions, and management tone. This type of information can influence position sizing or risk adjustments but cannot result in a thesis change until validated by Tier-1 results (product launch → actual revenue results). Quantum stocks experience a lot of Tier-3 (Noise) signals, such as unofficial or anecdotal information (e.g., rumors, hype, broad industry chatter). These signals are logged for context only.

D-Wave is expected to have a few upcoming catalysts that could serve as inflection events (e.g., potential closing of the South Korea system sale, further Advantage2 customer wins, strategic acquisitions, earnings reports). As for Unpredictability, revenue is lumpy, Advantage2 adoption is uncertain, and competitive surprises are possible. Regarding Control-Deficit, financial disclosure quality is good, but management hasn’t given numeric forward guidance. The Stakes are high. D-Wave trades at >150x EV/Sales (implying high expectations), and the company depends on a single technology class (annealing) for near-term revenues.

So, the next two quarters are event-heavy with low visibility, which results in a high “Tilt Index” and an increased risk of “precision drift” (rigid or noisy trading behaviors). To avoid making mistakes during uncertainty, investors ought to maintain a smaller position size. Any new Tier-1 events warrant a 48-hour “stability window” to ensure any investment decision is made with a cool head and full information. We want to avoid trading into volatility.

As for evidence bands, an example “bull band” would be >50% YoY revenue growth (signaling accelerating adoption beyond niche projections). On the flip side, a “bear band” may be <20% YoY growth (signaling stagnation or loss of momentum). Bookings growth may appear similar. Customer count is an important one, too. D-Wave’s customer count apparently fell from 130 in Q2 2024 to “in excess of 100 revenue-generating customers” in the latest quarter. An example bull band would be >25% YoY new customers, which is totally reasonable given the small base. Given these bands, the current results fall in the Base case, with a mix of signals. Top-line revenue growth is base, but the underlying demand (bookings) is weaker, as evidenced by the 1H 2025 bookings decline of 13% YoY.

Conclusion

To wrap up, D-Wave's ability to expand annealing beyond niche applications (e.g., Ford Otosan use case) remains a major question mark. Moreover, its foray into gated solutions is just a dream right now, with major tech companies hellbent on making breakthroughs in this space. In my view, there’s no good reason to believe D-Wave has any chance in gated solutions, which figure to require a substantial amount of R&D investments. D-Wave is clearly a high-risk, high-reward stock with a lot of noise that will contribute to price volatility.

Although I can see its utility in a barbell portfolio seeking exposure in quantum computing, given D-Wave’s premium valuation, I’d like to see better signals before jumping aboard (maintain “Sell”). Examples of signals include big customer wins, steady revenue growth, and progress on its gate-model initiative. In the meantime, investors may benefit from practicing some of the concepts outlined in the Dizzy framework.

data.title: Philadelphia Fed Manufacturing Index: Activity Weakened In August
data.description:
By Jennifer Nash

The Philly Fed's Manufacturing Business Outlook Survey is a monthly survey of about 250 manufacturers in the Third Federal Reserve District, which covers eastern Pennsylvania, southern New Jersey, and Delaware. Participants in the survey indicate the relative level of general business conditions in the region. Since this is a diffusion index, negative readings indicate contraction and positive ones indicate expansion. While it focuses exclusively on business in this district, this regional survey gives a reliable direction of the broader Chicago Fed's National Activity Index.

The latest Philadelphia Fed manufacturing index showed activity weakened in August, undoing most gains from last month. The index fell 16.2 points to -0.3 this month. The latest reading was worse than the forecast of 6.8.

Here is the summary from the survey:

Manufacturing activity in the region weakened this month, according to the firms responding to the August Manufacturing Business Outlook Survey. The current general activity index fell to a near-zero reading, the new orders index dipped into negative territory, and the shipments index also declined but remained positive. The employment index continued to suggest overall increases. Both price indexes remained elevated. The firms continued to expect growth over the next six months, and expectations were somewhat more widespread.

The first chart below gives us a look at this diffusion index since 2000, which shows us how it has behaved in proximity to the three 21st century recessions. The green dots show the indicator itself, which is quite noisy. Therefore, we've included the three-month moving average (purple line), which is more useful as an indicator of coincident economic activity. We can see longer and deeper periods of contraction during each of the recessions, with shallower contractions in '02, '03, '11, '12, '13, and '15. More recently, the index's 3-month moving average contracted from July 2022 to December 2023; however, no recession was called during that time.

In the next chart, we see the complete series, which dates from May 1960. For proof of the high volatility of the headline indicator, note that the average absolute monthly change across this data series is 8.2.

The next chart is an overlay of the General Activity Index and the Future General Activity Index — the outlook six months ahead.

Firms Continue to Expect Growth

The diffusion index for future general activity increased from 21.5 to 25.0 (see Chart). The future new orders index rose to 39.2, and the future shipments index rose to 40.3, their highest readings since May. Both future price indexes remained elevated but moved lower. The future capital expenditures index more than doubled, rising to 38.4, its highest reading since January.

For comparison, here is the latest ISM Manufacturing survey.

Let's compare all five Regional Manufacturing indicators. Here is a three-month moving average overlay of each since 2004 (for those with data).

Here is the same chart, including the average of the five. Readers will notice the range in expansion and contraction between all regions.

ETFs associated with industrials and manufacturing include: First Trust Industrials/Producer Durables AlphaDEX Fund (FXR), Industrial Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLI), Vanguard Industrials ETF (VIS), and iShares U.S. Industrials ETF (IYJ).

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

The comments below are not regulated with the same rigor as the rest of the site, and this is an 'enter at your own risk' area as discussion can get very heated. If you can't stand the heat... you know what they say...

We remove comments under the following categories:

  • Anti-Vaxxer or covid related misinformation
  • Stereotyping, prejudiced or racist language about individuals or the topic under discussion.
  • Inciting violence messages, encouraging hate groups and political violence.

Regardless of which side of the political divide you find yourself, please be courteous and don't direct abuse at other users.

For any issue with regards to comments please email us at : moderation@seekingalpha.com.

Our analysts are third party authors that include both professional investors and individual investors who may not be licensed or certified by any institute or regulatory body.

data.title: WM Technology Could Double
data.description:
I am cautious on cannabis stocks, but one that I really like is WM Technology (NASDAQ:MAPS). It has rallied a bit since I last wrote about it here at Seeking Alpha in June, suggesting it was a good stock for cannabis investors, but cannabis stocks have soared since then. In this follow-up, I reiterate my Strong Buy, discuss Q2 and the outlook, review the chart, and explain why I have a 20% position in my model portfolio at 420 Investor.

MAPS Had Good Financial Results in Q2

The company reported its Q2 on 8/7. Revenue of $44.8 million was down 2% from the prior year and in line with the guidance it had provided when it shared Q1 financial results. It had guided for adjusted EBITDA of $8 million, and the $11.7 million reported for Q2 exceeded its forecast and grew 16% from a year earlier. Operating cash flow was $11 million during Q2, compared to $5.7 million in Q1 and $12.7 million a year earlier. Capital spending of $6.5 million in H1 was less than the $16.7 million in operating cash flow.

In addition to good operational results, the company reported an even stronger balance sheet that has no debt. The cash increased to $59 million from $53.3 million at the end of Q1 and $52 million at year-end. A year earlier, it was $41.3 million. I use tangible book value to assess down-side risk, and the tangible book value of MAPS increased again to $57.8 million.

The Outlook Did Change in the Short-Term

In the press release, the company guided for revenue in Q3 of $41-43 million with adjusted EBITDA of $5-7 million. Both of these are down sequentially, and the revenue would decline by 8–12% from a year earlier. In 2024-Q3, reported adjusted EBITDA was $11.3 million, so the range provided for this quarter this year is quite lower but still positive.

MAPS is not widely followed by analysts. I am aware of two that currently provide research on it: Pablo Zuanic of Zuanic & Associates (formerly covering the cannabis industry at Cantor Fitzgerald) and Casey Ryan of Westpark. I have read Zuanic's research piece that he wrote after the quarter, providing updates, and I was able to access information about Ryan's outlook. Ryan apparently has a target of $2.61. Zuanic, who discloses that MAPS is a paying customer of his firm, changed his forecasts for 2025, 2026, and 2027:

While he cut his revenue estimates for 2025 and 2026 slightly, he boosted his 2027 outlook substantially. For adjusted EBITDA, he raised his 2025 projection, kept his 2026 forecast similar to his prior estimate, and boosted his 2027 outlook.

I like Zuanic's forecasts here. Before the Q2 report, I was assuming $30 million adjusted EBITDA for 2025 and then $35 million for 2026. My 2026 outlook for MAPS was based on a 17% margin for what I thought revenue could be that year (I was using the Koyfin consensus revenue of $211 million). Koyfin now has a consensus 2026 revenue of $188 million, and the 17% margin would be $32.0 million, slightly above Zuanic's forecast, which works out to a 16.9% margin on his own outlook. His boost in 2027 works out to a 16.9% margin as well.

Note that the company, which is reporting positive EPS now, has a consensus to report EPS of $0.16 in 2025, $0.17 in 2026, and $0.20 in 2027, according to Koyfin as well as Seeking Alpha.

MAPS Could Benefit From Cannabis Rescheduling

Cannabis rescheduling is not a done deal, but it could eliminate 280E taxation for American cannabis growers and sellers. 280E taxation has been weighing on cannabis companies, forcing them to pay income tax effectively that is much higher than normal tax rates.

Green Thumb Industries (OTCQX:GTBIF), which I like now relative to its peers, disclosed an effective tax rate of 96.7% in Q2 and 85.3% in H1. Many of its peers have income tax provisions that exceed their net income.

MAPS does not pay 280E taxes, but its customers are legally required to do so. They also struggle with debt, which has high interest rates and is tough to roll over.

If cannabis gets rescheduled from Schedule 1 to Schedule 3, 280E taxation would end. Of course, Congress could impose a new tax. So, it is not yet known that 280E taxation will be ending, but it would be very positive for spending by cannabis companies if it were to go away.

MAPS Is an Extremely Cheap Stock

MAPS has 107.2 million shares outstanding and also 49.3 million Class V shares. This totals 156.5 million. I do add 13.8 million Class P shares, 9.3 million RSUs, and 4.34 million PSUs to give a total effective share count of 184 million. There are 19.5 million $11.50 warrants outstanding too. At $1.12, I calculate a market cap of $206.1 million. Subtracting the cash, the enterprise value is $147.2 million.

Using the 2025 projected adjusted EBITDA, the enterprise value is just 4.1X Zuanic's 2025 projected adjusted EBITDA, which is very low for a NASDAQ-listed company that is operating federally legally. The 2025 PE is just 7X.

My target ahead of the Q2 report for year-end was $2.04, which was based on an enterprise value to projected adjusted EBITDA in 2026 of 8X. For year-end, adjusting for the lower projected adjusted EBITDA than I was assuming, the target would now be $1.65. While this is lower than my year-end target had been, it is 47% above the current price.

Today, I am sharing my look for the year ahead. I am using a multiple now of 10X and basing it on 50% of the 2026 projected adjusted EBITDA and 50% of the 2027 projected adjusted EBITDA. Using Zuanic's estimates, this works out to be projected adjusted EBITDA of $33.7 million. The target, assuming no change in shares outstanding or in cash (which could rise), is $2.15, 92% higher. Again, if 280E taxation goes away, revenue and adjusted EBITDA and EPS could increase for MAPS.

There are very few cannabis companies reporting profits these days, but MAPS is. My target a year out works out to be a 2026 PE of just 12.6X.

The MAPS Chart Says Buy

MAPS is down 18.8% in 2025, which is worse than the market. The New Cannabis Ventures Global Cannabis Stock Index, which does include MAPS, has increased 2.5% so far this year. Here is the one-year chart for MAPS:

Let me start with the negative: MAPS did gap up on 8/11, when the market started to process again potential rescheduling of cannabis. This gap is down to $0.95. Of course there is another gap down to $0.77 from November 2024, when the company reported its Q3.

There is a lot of good that I see, but first I need to remind readers about the big run-up that started in December 2024, when the company announced that insiders had $1.70 bid. I was not recommending the stock then and never believed that the transaction would take place. It was dropped officially in June after I wrote my Strong Buy piece. I said in that article that it was a risk that the deal would not go through.

The low last November of $0.70 was not the all-time low, which was $0.60 in early 2023. I do like the higher lows that have been set over the past year. I see support currently at $0.90-.95 and note that the 50-day moving average, just below $1.00, could provide support too.

Cannabis stocks have soared since 8/11, and there was some run-up ahead of then. Looking at the action since 6/30, the Global Cannabis Stock Index has increased 40.4%. The Q3 gains have been very high for MSOs, with the AdvisorShares Pure US Cannabis ETF (MSOS) more than doubling, a much-bigger increase than MAPS enjoyed:

MSOS has seen its share count increase by 26% so far in Q3. It had experienced some redemptions earlier in the year. I think that MSOS, which I have rated Strong Sell, is very risky. If 280E does not go away, it will likely face redemptions again.

There Are Risks to MAPS

While MAPS has closed above $1 since 8/11. It's testing that level and could face delisting if it can't hold it. It did not receive a warning in July or August, but NASDAQ rules state that if a stock closes below $1.00 for 30 consecutive business days, it can be delisted. The process takes 180 days from when the company is warned, and it can be extended with an appeal. MAPS did receive a warning in October 2024 but was able to close above $1.00 within 180 days.

Another potential challenge to MAPS is that if 280E taxation remains, its customer base is very weak and may continue to resist spending for its technology. MAPS has not made any attempt to move beyond the cannabis industry.

I don't think that either of these risks should keep investors away from the stock, but both could weigh on the stock, making it get even cheaper.

Conclusion

MAPS serves a very challenged industry, and it has held up well this quarter, rising 25% in price. The stocks of cannabis companies have soared recently on the news of potential cannabis rescheduling, but MAPS has lagged. The federally legal NASDAQ-listed stock seems cheap, and the company has no debt burdens. My target is much higher than the current price and could be far exceeded if 280E taxation goes away.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: D-Wave Quantum: Too Much Cash To Ignore
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Markets are heading into the last lap of the Q2 earnings season, having put up a reasonable performance so far through the quarter.

But the current chatter about the AI bubble has put a lid on the enthusiasm markets had demonstrated at the start of this month. Getting caught in the crosshairs of the concerns surrounding the AI bubble are the four biggest quantum computing stocks, which typically also get attached to the broader trade of emerging technologies like AI.

In this past week, during which concerns about the AI bubble grew, the four big quantum stocks fell and are poised to collectively shed at least $2B in market capitalization.

Of the four quantum stocks, D-Wave Quantum Inc. (NYSE:QBTS) got hit the hardest, falling over 11% week-to-date. But that could also be because D-Wave’s stock is scoring the biggest YTD gains for the year, up 79%, outpacing every other quantum stock by a large margin.

There is one other area that D-Wave outperforms the others—cash.

This could be a huge tailwind for D-Wave, as I discuss below, but there are some risks due to which I advise a Hold rating for the company.

Quick Primer On D-Wave Quantum’s Business Model

In the current state of computing, as we know it, information is created, processed, and consumed to provide value. The basic units of information that are created are called bits, and over time, computing systems have rapidly evolved, becoming more powerful and efficient at solving computational problems based on bits.

In quantum computing, this can all be sped up much faster due to the creation of qubits, the quantum equivalent of bits, but it needs quantum computing systems to create and process qubits. Today, the four most popular pure-play quantum computing companies publicly listed on markets have their own approaches to quantum computing.

D-Wave differentiates itself from the rest of the pure-play quantum space in three ways.

First is D-Wave’s unique approach to solving the quantum computational problem by leaning on a process called quantum annealing that helps the company and its clients solve optimization problems using quantum compute methods. The company follows a cloud software-style business model where it sells access to its quantum systems via the QCaaS (quantum compute as a service).

Second, D-Wave prides itself on being the only publicly listed pure-play quantum company that generates revenue from commercial sales of its quantum systems, unlike its peers that still heavily rely on government grants and endowments. It counts companies such as Mastercard (MA) as its clients.

And third is D-Wave’s massive cash hoard, at least 50% bigger than any of its peers.

D-Wave’s Cash Hoard Is Too Big Too Ignore

After factoring in the Q2 ER updates from all the pure-play quantum compute companies, I observe that D-Wave has worked up the biggest cash war chest of all—$819M in cash and ST investments. This is a record cash hoard for D-Wave and is at least 50% higher than the cash held by any of its peers. IonQ, Inc. (IONQ), a quantum peer, comes the closest to D-Wave with its own cash stockpile of $547M in cash.

The record cash D-Wave has secured for itself this year cushions the company from the $112M in annualized expenses, gross and operating costs, it burns through the year.

Per my estimates of annualized costs and current cash volumes on its balance sheet, D-Wave’s cash creates a strong runway of ~97 months of cash it can burn, which is a significant cushion for the company, in my opinion.

On the Q2 call with analysts, D-Wave’s management advised that part of the reason to build up its cash pile was to give the company some runway in ramping up expenses for four main areas—R&D, wafer fabrication & chip packaging, sales & marketing, and infrastructure.

But there is a strong reason why the company is planning to ramp up expenses. I noted earlier that D-Wave is possibly the only company to see commercial viability in its QCaaS business model, and their Q2 ER showed that bookings jumped 92% y/y to $1.3M, accelerating from both the previous Q1 CY25 quarter and the Q2 CY24 year. The combination of y/y acceleration and sequential acceleration is the holy grail of growth that markets look for, and D-Wave demonstrated that in the Q2 quarter.

The company did reveal that most of the bookings involve companies signing small contracts to get access to D-Wave’s QCaaS quantum system to evaluate the feasibility of applying D-Wave’s quantum computing systems to their business problems. If the dollar value of contracts that D-Wave’s management implies were just “small amounts,” then D-Wave must have signed on a large volume of new labels due to the 92% jump in bookings that they delivered in Q2.

Also, management did categorically state that they plan to deploy a part of their $819M in cash towards pursuing their M&A strategy, which has “now become a strategic priority for the company.”

D-Wave definitely looks well positioned to grab this opportunity that lies ahead of them to win more commercial deals and push the barriers of what is commercially possible with their QCaaS business model, especially with the strong cash hoard that they have.

D-Wave’s Setup Looks Interesting From A Valuation Standpoint

In response to the strong performance that D-Wave has put up through this year, analysts have raised their estimates on D-Wave, with consensus estimates calling for D-Wave’s CY25 revenues to be ~162% y/y, at the midpoint of those consensus estimates.

For that kind of expected growth, D-Wave trades at a forward valuation (EV/revenues) of 182x CY25 revenues.

If I compare D-Wave’s current sales multiple & growth pace to NVIDIA’s (NVDA) pricing scenario in CY23, D-Wave could be extremely expensive. Through 2023, when Nvidia’s revenues were expected to at least double, Nvidia’s highest revenue multiple was just 23x forward revenues. So D-Wave definitely looks way overvalued vs. Nvidia.

Note: I used Nvidia as a comparison because D-Wave’s CEO talked about the comparison of possibly using D-Wave’s quantum chip as an alternative to Nvidia’s GPUs.

But on an apples-to-apples basis, D-Wave’s sales multiple of 175x looks a lot more reasonable vs. its pure-play quantum peers.

However, D-Wave does trade at a higher book value multiple of 7.6x relative to itself, and I would have preferred if the book value multiple were trading lower than mid-single digits, especially since management has advised investors that most of that cash is going to be used this year.

With cash set to decrease due to deployment this year, D-Wave’s value of assets on its balance sheet will be pressured, eventually stretching the book value multiple, which is not something I would recommend a buy on just yet.

Then, there is also the added risk that if business does not materialize down the road like management is planning for today, they will have to again go to the markets and dilute current shareholders. D-Wave has been diluting its current shareholder base by raising more capital via secondary share sales, as can be seen in the table below.

Tickers

YTD Dilution

CY24 Dilution

3Yr CAGR Dilution

QBTS

57.2%

39.2%

269.29%

(QUBT)

18.0%

41.0%

49.21%

(RGTI)

61.3%

40.0%

98.95%

IONQ

17.8%

5.2%

15.24%

So, while D-Wave appears to be doing the right things, there definitely are some risks that I am not entirely comfortable with taking on.

Takeaway

D-Wave Computing looks like one of the best picks of the pure-play quantum computing companies available on public markets today. They have a relatively stronger appeal in terms of the commercial viability of their quantum computing systems and services versus their peers and are demonstrating strong growth in bookings. The robust volumes of cash on their balance sheet make this stock difficult to ignore.

But the risks associated so far may put pressure on the valuation of D-Wave, leading me to advocate for a Hold rating on the company.

data.title: Cracker Barrel's Logo Change Misses The Bigger Picture
data.description:
In the last five days alone, Cracker Barrel Old Country Store stock (NASDAQ:CBRL) has fallen by almost 12%. The main reason for this is the rebranding, which was not well received on social media.

Despite the discussions, this seems to me to have become a little too “biased”. Over the past five years, CBRL stock has accumulated a performance of -54%, which is a reflection of the fundamentals. A business overhaul seems like the right move to me. Looking at the financials, it is clear that something needed to be done to try to turn the company around, as was done with Brinker International's Chili's (EAT).

But even though the reaction seems exaggerated to me and I believe that something needs to be done, I don't think we can give them the benefit of the doubt, at least yet.

Cracker Barrel Needed Change

Personally, I found Cracker Barrel's previous logo more charming, but the truth is that this is practically irrelevant to my analysis. The first question to ask is: if so many people liked the previous model, why was the business in decline? In my view, a logo change alone cannot save a business or bury it.

But what is a little clearer to me is that the company should not have stayed the way it was (and here I am not referring to the logo or the political context, but to the business).

Cracker Barrel receives an F in growth and a C- in profitability according to quant ratings. And that's no surprise. The long-term charts below clearly reveal this. For some time now, revenue has been growing at a very slow pace, margins are under a lot of pressure, and free cash flow is very low. This reflects a company that is no longer doing so well operationally, struggling to remain relevant.

What is the solution? Well, it goes far beyond the logo. Taking Chili's as an example, much has been done to improve financials, ranging from marketing to a better value proposition, as well as technology and culinary innovations.

In its presentation, Cracker Barrel explained its plans for restructuring. The initiatives involve refining the brand, but also include more important points such as the menu, value proposition, store remodeling, becoming more relevant in the digital realm, and providing a better experience for employees too.

It is still too early to say whether the implementation of these initiatives will be successful, but some positive points can already be seen. In Q2, the company reported comparable store restaurant sales of 4.7%, which the company attributed to these reforms, as mentioned in the earnings call:

“As these initiatives exemplify how we're evolving the brand by leaning into what makes Cracker Barrel great and doing so in a refined way that appeals to both existing and new guests alike. We're excited about our progress and our teams are energized. Looking back at Q3, the quarter started soft. So we took actions to support the top line and tightly manage our expenses without limiting our ability to deliver our important fourth quarter initiative. [...]

From a culinary perspective, our spring promotion featured 2 shrimp dishes, a bold Louisiana-style shrimp skillet and a comforting Shrimp n' Grits skillet. We also expanded our pancake platform by introducing innovative new flavors and options across various price points as part of our broader barbell strategy. From an operational perspective, we remain focused on strong execution and the metrics that matter [...]”

Considering the financials, all these positive operational metrics and the hints from management in the earnings call already suggest to me that the business is improving.

Will the Logo Change Affect the Business?

With that in mind, I think the market's reaction to the logo change was misguided. In my view, the negative repercussions alone are not enough to be sure that this will impact the company, and at the same time, it makes it seem that a change in the business is not welcome.

Not every change is welcome, and it still depends on the management's execution, but if the initiatives result in better KPIs, this will increase the financials and consequently bring more shareholder value. Regarding the negative repercussions, I believe that only in the coming quarters we will know if this really materialized in lower traffic to the stores or if it was something that stayed more on the internet.

Is It Worth Giving Cracker Barrel The Benefit Of The Doubt?

Although the changes have already shown some positive signs, it is undeniable that Cracker Barrel faces numerous uncertainties. It may be that it will indeed manage to retain more customers and eventually find a more profitable model, just as the reaction on social media may already be a sign that strong growth may not come.

So I don't think it's worth giving Cracker Barrel the benefit of the doubt just yet. It seems much more like a “show me” story to me. And beyond the uncertainties, one of the issues is valuation.

After the decline, the multiples went to much more reasonable levels, such as a forward P/E of 17x, but for a company in turnaround, that seems rich to me.

One way to see if the company is really cheap is to look at its history. The maximum net income margin ever reached was just over 9%, but the average for the last 10 years is much closer to 5%.

Taking the current revenue of $3.5 billion and the margin of 5%, we have a net income of $175 million. If the company manages to return to this, it will be trading at around 7x earnings. In other words, if management manages to do this in 1 or 2 years, and the fair multiple is 14x, this means an implied upside of 100%.

If the company eventually manages to return to this margin and maintain healthy top-line growth in the high single digits, then yes, there is substantial upside to be unlocked. And remember, companies are living entities; they change all the time. Just as they changed their logo, they can revert to the old one, undo the changes, and so on.

But even with this apparent asymmetry, uncertainties detract from the compelling side of the case. I prefer to give a neutral rating and see how the company will fare in this chaotic scenario in the coming quarters, even if it means losing a little profitability if everything goes well.

Therefore, my rating is a hold.
